Summary: MATLAB crashes or coverage missing when analyzing subsystem containing Switch Case block
Description: This bug occurs when a Model Coverage analysis is scoped to a particular subsystem (for example, by setting the CovPath parameter) where Switch Case blocks are found both inside and outside of this subsystem. Under this circumstance, either MATLAB crashes or the analysis completes but the Switch Case blocks inside of the specified subsystem are incorrectly reported as never having executed for coverage.
Workaround: Do not scope the coverage analysis to a single subsystem if this subsystem contains a Switch Case block while one or more Switch Case blocks are found elsewhere in the model.? Instead analyze coverage for the entire model by ensuring that the CovPath parameter is set to the default value '/'.

Summary: USRP support package installation can error out when installing the dd v0.5 for Windows third-party software
Description: Installing the USRP support package on Windows can trigger an error such as this: Error installing third party software dd v0.5 for Windows: Error executing command "unzip('C:\MATLAB\SupportPackages\R2014b\downloads\usrpradio_download\dd-0.5.zip', fullfile(sdruroot,'uhdapps','utils'))" Invalid zip file "C:\MATLAB\SupportPackages\R2014b\downloads\usrpradio_download\dd-0.5.zip".
Workaround: Download the USRP support package to a folder (for example, C:\MATLAB\SupportPackages\R2014b\downloads\usrpradio_download).In the folder, delete the dd-0.5.zip file. It is not a valid zip file.Use a web browser to download dd-0.5.zip manually from http://www.chrysocome.net/downloads/dd-0.5.zip to C:\MATLAB\SupportPackages\R2014b\downloads\usrpradio_download.Install the USRP support package from the folder C:\MATLAB\SupportPackages\R2014b\downloads\usrpradio_download.

Summary: Simulink does not display text exceeding 35 lines
Description: If the text of a model element exceeds 35 lines, the text beyond 35 lines is not displayed. The text still exists internally and Simulink uses it. You may not see all of the text present in the model. While editing the text of the model element, the Simulink editor displays the entirety of the text.

Summary: MATLAB crash while generating annotations for a saturating add or subtraction operation
Description: MATLAB might crash during code generation for a model under the following the conditions: For certain blocks, the Saturate on overflow  checkbox is selected. On the Code Generation > Comments  pane, the Operator annotations  check box is selected.  The crash occurs during code generation while generating annotations for a saturating add or subtraction operation.
Workaround: In the Configuration Parameters dialog, on the Code Generation > Comments pane, clear the Operator annotations check box.

Summary: Crash in GLBridge when shutting down MATLAB
Description: When shutting down MATLAB, after using Simulink, sometimes MATLAB can crash.  Clicking the "Details" button in the crash dialog shows that a segmentation violation has occurred, and the first line of the call stack shows "GLBridge::GLBridge".
Workaround: The problem is caused by an out-of-date toolboxcache file.  Restart MATLAB and run this command: >> rehash toolboxcache.This action repairs the toolboxcache file and prevents the problem from occurring again.

Summary: For models with simulation tunable, non-scalar parameters, derived minimums and maximums can be incorrect
Description: Fixed-Point Tool can collect derived minimums and maximums for a model. For some models with simulation tunable parameters that are non-scalar, a bug may cause the derived ranges to be incorrectly narrow. Simulation tunable parameters are parameters whose storage class has been defined as one of the following: SimulinkGlobal ExportedGlobal ImportedExtern ImportedExternPointer Custom  Parameters that are scalar or non-simulation-tunable will not trigger this bug. To find if your model contains parameters that might trigger this bug, download the attachment and run the included MATLAB function findAffectedParams_979671.m  included. ?

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: If a parfor-loop writes to a function input argument, the MATLAB? Coder? software can generate incorrect code or produce incorrect MEX functions.
Description: If a parfor -loop writes to a function input argument, the MATLAB?  Coder? software replaces references to the input argument with references to uninitialized memory. The reference to unitialized memory can produce wrong answers and can result in SEGv's in MATLAB or in the generated application code.

Summary: On a 64-bit machine, version R2013a requires a 64-bit version of MATLAB?
Description: When Polyspace is used with a 32-bit version of MATLAB, a verification cannot be started. It fails with the followng error:  ### Computing code verification options The system cannot find the specified path ### Aborted: Operation failed  This error is specific to version 2013a.

Summary: MATLAB crashes if SimEvents Attribute Function block has more than 32 input or output arguments
Description: If you create a SimEvents model containing an Attribute Function block with more than 32 input or output arguments, MATLAB crashes upon model compilation.
Workaround: To work around this issue, split the function computation across multiple Attribute Function blocks, each with less than 32 input and output arguments. Connect the blocks in series to create a computation that is equivalent to the original.

Summary: MATLAB might crash when SimEvents compiles a model containing Replicate or Entity Splitter block with a large number of output ports
Description: MATLAB might run out of memory and crash when SimEvents compiles a model containing a Replicate or Entity Splitter block with both of these conditions: The entity output parameter of the block is set to Any entity output port is available . The block has many output ports that are ultimately connected to queues or servers that block entities when their storage is full. The number of Replicate or Entity Splitter output ports that causes MATLAB to run out memory depends on the overall number of entity paths in your model and the amount of memory available on your computer.
Workaround: To work around this issue, do one of the following:If possible, change the entity output parameter of the Replicate or Entity Splitter block to All entity output ports are available. This configuration greatly reduces the number of calculations (and hence memory usage) performed within SimEvents to track distinct entity paths.Reduce the number of output ports of the Replicate or Entity Splitter block that are connected to entity-blocking storage blocks by modifying the storage blocks to have infinite storage (e.g. use an Infinite Server block or specify a queue of infinite capacity). This will reduce the memory required by SimEvents to keep track of distinct entity paths.

Summary: Model build error when custom code filename contains a hyphen
Description: If a model is configured with custom code using the Code Generation > Custom Code  pane of the Configuration Parameters dialog box, and if a specified custom code filename contains a hyphen (for example, custom-code.c ), a model rebuild might fail with an error similar to the following: LINK : ... cannot open input file 'custom.obj'  The error reflects that the hyphen in the custom code filename was incorrectly converted to a space. In the example shown above, the build is looking for filename custom  rather than the correct custom-code .
Workaround: To work around this bug, rename the custom code file so that it does not contain a hyphen, and update the Code Generation > Custom Code pane to reflect the rename.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Code generation from MATLAB can produce wrong code for a call to a function with multiple-outputs with persistent variable as input.
Description: Code generation from MATLAB?  can produce a wrong answer for a function call meeting the following conditions: 1. The called function has more than one output. 2. The function call uses the same variable as input and output for two or more  input/output pairs, for example: [x y] = foo(x, y) ; 3. At least one of the input variables passed to the called function is a persistent variable declared in the calling function. 4. In the called function, the inputs are directly copied to the outputs. 5. The variables passed as inputs are allocated on the heap due to their unknown or large size. In the following example, the function call from main  to bar  can produce a wrong answer in the generated code: function [a,b] = main(n)  persistent x y; x = ones(n,1); y = zeros(n,1); [x,y] = bar(x,y); a = x; b = y;end  function [x, y] = bar(x, y) x(1) = 10; y(1) = 100;end
Workaround: Rewrite the called function so that the input and output variables:are not the sameare in memory throughout the function callIn the following example, the coder.ceval calls cause x and y to remain in memory until the end of the call to the function bar.function [x1, y1] = bar(x, y)x1 = x;y1 = y;x(1) = 10;y(1) = 100;coder.ceval('void'), coder.ref(x));coder.ceval('void'), coder.ref(y));end

Summary: Several blocks do not handle latitude values outside the +90 and -90 degree range
Description: Using the following blocks with latitude input values outside the +90 and -90 degree range can lead to incorrect output values: NRLMSISE-00 Atmosphere Model Geoid Height WGS84 Gravity Model Geodetic to Geocentric Latitude Geocentric to Geodetic Latitude LLA to ECEF Position
Workaround: As a workaround, before passing latitude values to these blocks, preprocess these input values to make sure that they are within the +90 and -90 degree range. For example, if a location is described as 100 degrees latitude and 10 degrees longitude, the equivalent position is 80 degrees latitude and -170 degrees longitude.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect fixed-point scaling applied to expressions passed into fixed-point Code Replacement functions
Description: Incorrect fixed-point scaling might be applied to expressions passed into functions under the following conditions: The function operates on fixed-point types with non-zero fraction lengths The function with matching types is registered in the Code Replacement Library The function is passed an expression such as a+b  that evaluates to a type with a non-zero fraction length  Under the above conditions the generated code applies fixed-point scaling to the result of the expression prior to passing that expression to the replacement function. For example y = fixed_point_sqrt( (u1+u2) << some_fraction_length ); might be emitted in the generated code rather than the expected y = fixed_point_sqrt(u1+u2);
Workaround: To avoid the undesired scaling on the expression, for your TflEntry that maps to your fixed-point function, set the property AcceptExprInput to false.hEnt = RTW.TflCFunctionEntry;setTflCFunctionEntryParameters( hEnt, ... 'Key', 'sqrt', ... 'ImplementationName', 'replacement_sqrt', ...'AcceptExprInput', false);

Summary: The radiation pattern of crossed dipole antenna is oriented in the wrong direction
Description: The phased.CrossedDipoleAntennaElement  System object models a crossed dipole in y-z plane. Therefore, the main lobe of its radiation pattern should point in the positive "x" direction. Instead, it points in the positive "z" direction.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: For special cases of mismatched fixed-point slope and bias data types, additions and subtractions lose saturation protection in Stateflow and MATLAB
Description: For special combinations of fixed-point slope and bias data types, additions and subtractions in Stateflow or MATLAB lose requested saturation protection. Incorrect removal of protection will cause overflows to wrap instead of saturate. This bug does not  affect: The Simulink Sum block. MATLAB calculations that are not using code generation technologies. Calculations that use only integer or binary-point scaling.  The bug occurs only when all  of the following conditions are true: The output or one of the inputs has a non-zero Bias or a non-trivial SlopeAdjustmentFactor. The inputs and output all have Slopes in the range [1.0, 2.0). The three slopes are not identical or the net bias is not zero. The calculation is specified to saturate. Overflow is possible for the operation. Overflow would not have been possible if all the scaling was trivial.  If all these conditions are met, then incorrect removal of overflow protection from the addition or subtraction operation might occur. This might lead to incorrect behavior of many features including the following: Fixed-point simulations using buildInstrumentedMex and fiaccel MATLAB Coder generated mex and C code Fixed-point derived range analysis Stateflow simulations MATLAB Function Block simulations Embedded Coder generated C code  This is an example of code that is exposed to this bug. fm = fimath(... 'OverflowAction', 'Saturate', 'RoundingMethod', 'Floor', ... 'CastBeforeSum', true, 'SumMode', 'SpecifyPrecision', ... 'SumWordLength', 8, 'SumFractionLength', 0); u1=fi(190.5,0,7,1.5,0,fm); u2=fi(127,0,7,1.0,0.0,fm); y=u1+u2 When MATLAB interpreter does the computation, the ideal answer floor(317.5) is saturated to 255 as requested. When the same code is executed using code generation technologies, the bug incorrectly removes the saturation logic. The ideal answer floor(317.5) incorrectly wraps around to 61.
Workaround: Precast each input to the operation type. In the attached example, the input types have non-radix scaling and the output type is int32. Casting each input to int32 should result in correct overflow behavior. See attached models mIncorrectSaturatingAddWorkaroundFcnBlk.mdl and mIncorrectSaturatingAddWorkaroundSF.mdl for examples of this workaround.

Summary: Simulation And Code Generation Mismatch: On 40-bit target hardware, incorrect multiplication helper functions may be generated
Description: This bug occurs only when all  the following conditions are true: The size of the largest supported integer on the target hardware is 40 bits The generated code contains 32-bit multiplication helper functions  The following conditions increase the risk of this bug: Any non-binary point scaling on any type (including Data Type Conversion block, Sum block, etc) Use of a Lookup Table Radix multiplication with input type word lengths less than or equal to 32 bits and output type with word length greater than 32 bits Multiplication with signed 32-bit input types and unsigned 32-bit output with saturation on overflow  ?
Workaround: A workaround is to convert input data types to 40-bit word length.The attached model, "IncorrectMultiplicationHelperPreCastWorkaround.mdl", demonstrates casting of the input to 40-bit word length while maintaining the scaling.This model contains a subsystem that uses operations which may trigger this bug, including a cast, saturating multiplication, multiplication with output greater than 32 bits, and an addition with non-binary point scaling on the output.

Description: When Simulink references approximately 100 or more models from a top model, Rapid Accelerator mode simulations with RapidAcceleratorUpToDateCheck set to off can experience a significant slowdown in the initialization phase. Simulation can be 10x to 20x slower than simulation in Accelerator mode, not including up-to-date checks for referenced models. This comparison does not factor the initial build times for both modes.

Summary: Diagnostic check is over-restrictive on asynchronous function call system in action system.
Description: When an asynchronous subsystem (driven by an asynchronous initiator) is put inside an action or iterator subsystem, Simulink detects the mismatch and generates an error. This behavior is normal. However, when the diagnostic is over-restrictive, Simulink can also generate an error for any of the following cases when an action/iterator subsystem contains a normal function call subsystem: 1. Action/Iterator subsystem is in an asynchronous function call subsystem. 2. Action/Iterator subsystem is in an AUTOSAR runnable. 3. Export Function feature has Action/Iterator subsystems. In the above cases, Simulink can incorrectly error out on modeling patterns that should work.

Summary: Several functions do not handle latitude values outside the +90 and -90 degree range
Description: Using the following functions with latitude input values outside the +90 and -90 degree range can lead to incorrect output values:  atmosnrlmsise00  geod2geoc  geoc2geod  flat2lla  lla2flat  geoidheight  gravitywgs84
Workaround: As a workaround, before passing latitude values to these functions, preprocess these input values to make sure that they are within the +90 and -90 degree range. For example, if a location is described as 100 degrees latitude and 10 degrees longitude, the equivalent position is 80 degrees latitude and -170 degrees longitude.

However, Stateflow Charts containing MATLAB action language use one-based vector indexing. Summary: For referenced models, a warning or error may be issued when the parameter MAT-file logging is set to on, even when the parameter is not visible on the Configuration Parameters dialog box
Description: Generating code for a model that contains a Model block causes a warning or error if both  these conditions exist: The Configuration Parameters > Diagnostics > Model Referencing > Model configuration mismatch  is either set to error  or warning . The Configuration Parameters > Code Generation > System Target File  parameter is set to either xpctarget.tlc  or rsim.tlc .  Under these conditions, a warning or error is issued regarding the Configuration Parameters > Code Generation > Interface > MAT-file logging  parameter. This parameter is not visible on the Configuration Parameters dialog box for these targets and has the setting on  by default. The value for this parameter must be set to off  for the referenced model. However, you cannot change the value from the Configuration Parameters dialog.
Workaround: Set the Configuration Parameters > Diagnostics > Model Referencing > Model configuration mismatch parameter to none.

Summary: Simulation And Code Generation Mismatch: The Divide block can produce inconsistent results when computing reciprocals that mix single precision with integer or fixed-point.
Description: This bug occurs only if the Divide block is configured as follows: "Number of inputs" is set to "/", or "//" followed by any combination of "/" and "*". The first input port and the output port have different data types. One of the two data types must be single. The other data type must be boolean, integer or fixed-point. Single must be able to represent all values of the second data type without precision loss. A necessary condition is for the second data type to have a word length less than 26 bits.  Inconsistent results occur depending on the mode. In normal mode simulations, the reciprocal will be computed using only single precision. In accelerated simulations and in any form of code generation, the reciprocal will use double precision for intermediate computations. The Divide blocks in the attached "Reciprocal" model satisfy these conditions. The first referenced model computes the reciprocal through normal mode simulation. The second referenced model uses Accelerator mode, so it computes the reciprocal through code generation. For the same input values, the normal mode simulation and accelerated simulation results produce inconsistent output values.
Workaround: To get consistent results for all forms of simulation and for code generation, begin by deciding which of the two computation approaches meets the needs of your application.Strict single: use only single precisionMaximum precision: use double precision for intermediate calculationsFor maximum precision, insert a Data Type Conversion block in front of the product blocks first input. Set the output data type of the inserted conversion block to double.For strict single, the key is to have the Divide block's first input and output both use the data type single. It will be necessary to insert a Data Type Conversion block either before or after the Divide block. For strict single, if the output is already single, then insert a Data Type Conversion block in front of the Divide block's first input. Set the output data type of this inserted conversion block to single.For strict single, if the input is already single, then insert a Data Type Conversion block after the Divide block. Copy the Divide block's original data type and paste it to the output data type of this inserted conversion block. Finally, change the output data type of the Divide block to single.The attached "WorkAroundReciprocal" model shows the workaround for the "Reciprocal" model discussed in the "Description" section. You can use the script, "findMixTypedReciprocal", to automatically detect Divide blocks in your model that might have this issue.

Summary: Large number of functions called by generated main can cause crash during compilation.
Description: When the number of functions called by the generated main is large, the verification can fail with the message:Stubbing unknown functions ... *** Spawne error (binary=C:\MATLAB\R2013b_bash\bin\win64\ps_c_stubber.exe). Retrying...*** Spawne error (binary=C:\MATLAB\R2013b_bash\bin\win64\ps_c_stubber.exe). Retrying...*** Spawne error (binary=C:\MATLAB\R2013b_bash\bin\win64\ps_c_stubber.exe). Retrying...*** Spawne error (binary=C:\MATLAB\R2013b_bash\bin\win64\ps_c_stubber.exe). Retrying...*** Spawne error (binary=C:\MATLAB\R2013b_bash\bin\win64\ps_c_stubber.exe). Exiting. System error No such file or directory (noent: No such file or directory) Error: unable to execute C:\MATLAB\R2013b_bash\bin\win64\ps_c_stubber.exe (errno=2).        Size of all the arguments passed to the binary exceed system limits (32767). Exactly how many functions is considered as large depends on the length of the function names. The number is typically over 500. The failure is more likely when the verification is run on code generated from a Simulink model, where the number of functions can be important.The error occurs only in Windows.
Workaround: Since the failure occurs due to a large number of functions given to the main generator, a workaround is to specify the parameter 'all' to -functions-called-in-loop or -main-generator-calls, instead of an explicit list of functions.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Sum block: Simulation and code generation may give different answers if one input value is much bigger than the others and the inputs are a mix of real and complex floating point numbers.
Description: For floating point operations, the expression "a-a-b" doesn't always equate to "-a-b+a". When the Sum block is configured as '--+', simulation makes the port with plus sign as the first port in the expression to compute the output i.e. it uses "a-a-b". However, code generation uses "-a-b+a" to compute output. Hence, these algorithms may produce different results for such floating point inputs.
Workaround: To work around this problem, make the port with plus sign as the first input port.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Simulink? Coder? can generate empty functions for a model with Variant Subsystem blocks
Description: Simulink Coder can generate empty functions for a model with Variant Subsystem blocks, if one of the variants contains a block that generates code using a TLC implementation.  User S-function blocks, and library blocks implemented with an S-function often use TLC to implement code generation.

Summary: Code generation error when the output of get is passed as input to numerictype in a MATLAB Function block.
Description: Suppose that in a MATLAB Function block, you use get  to get the value of a property of a fi  object and then pass that value to the numerictype  constructor function. Code generation fails with the error message: All inputs to this function must be constant . For example: fraclength = get(u, 'FractionLength');   nT = numerictype(1, 16, fraclength + 1);
Workaround: Instead of using the get function, make a copy of the numerictype object associated with the fi object and use dot notation to get the value of the property. For example:Tu = numerictype(u);nT = numerictype(1, 16, Tu.FractionLength + 1);

However, in R2013a, it does. This incorrect behavior can cause errors if the set  method is called in a context where MATLAB does not expect a return argument. Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: 1-D, 2-D, n-D Lookup Table and Prelookup blocks: incorrect code generation for evenly spaced breakpoint searches with certain combinations of input vs. breakpoint data types.
Description: Incorrect code is generated when all of the following conditions exist simultaneously on a Prelookup, 1-D, 2-D, or n-D Lookup block: Block parameter 'Index Search Method' is set to 'Evenly spaced points'. For the Prelookup block, parameter 'BreakpointDataTypeStr' is set to a fixed-point data type with a non-zero exponent, e.g, fixdt(1,16,2^2) . The breakpoint data can also come via an input port to the Prelookup block for later releases.For 1-D, 2-D, or n-D Lookup blocks, the parameter name pattern for the breakpoint data type string is 'BreakpointsForDimension[k]DataTypeStr' where [k] is a string of an integer between 1 and 30, e.g., 'BreakpointsForDimension2DataTypeStr'You can access the breakpoint data types in the block's dialog box. The corresponding input signal data type is floating point, i.e., the input type resolves to single or double.  When your model contains the settings that trigger the bug, the problem will manifest itself in all code generation usages including Simulink Accelerator, Rapid Accelerator, Simulink Coder, and Embedded Coder. The generated code will compile and produce incorrect results. Please see the attached example models to understand when incorrect results will occur. Note: Detection and repair functions with example models are attached to this report to help understand the problem, detect if any of your models are affected by this bug, and apply a workaround to the affected blocks.
Workaround: There are several workarounds, you can choose any one of the following for each affected block:Set 'BreakpointsForDimension[k]DataTypeStr' (or 'BreakpointDataTypeStr' for the Prelookup block) to be same as the input port data typeStore the breakpoint parameter in a Simulink.Parameter object, set its DataType property to be the same as the inport data type, and set the data object as the BreakpointData parameterCast the input data type to be the same as the breakpoint data type by inserting a Data Type Conversion block on the line feeding the input port of the affected blockSet the IndexSearchMethod parameter to 'Binary Search' for affected blocks.?

Summary: VideoReader fails on Windows? 7 SP1 when when reading WMV files that have dynamic format changes
Description: On Windows? 7 and higher, the VideoReader  object creation fails for some WMV files that report dynamic format changes. Summary: Subsystems with multiplexed asynchronous function calls cannot use an external timer through LibManageAsyncCounter.
Description: When multiple asynchronous initiators call an asynchronous function call subsystem, Simulink does not allow code in such a subsystem to use an external timer. You must  get the absolute time from the base rate timer because:  1. The asynchronous initiators can specify conflicting timer sources. 2. Even if the time type of the timer source were to be the same, every asynchronous initiator can specify different timer attributes such as data type and resolution. 3. Sharing a timer across different asynchronous tasks can cause timer value corruption. This restriction can cause some models to stop using the external timer.

Summary: Building a static library from a Simulink Model or MATLAB script using the LCC toolchain on a 32-bit Windows machine fails in MATLAB 8.1 (R2013a)
Description: When building a static library on 32-bit Windows computer with LCC compiler, the following build error can happen: "### Created: ./readfile.lib" process_begin: CreateProcess(NULL, rm -f readfile.lnk, ...) failed.make (e=2): The system cannot find the file specified. gmake: *** [readfile.lib] Error 2 The make command returned an error of 2 'An_error_occurred_during_the_call_to_make' is not recognized as an internal or external command, operable program or batch file. Error(s) encountered while building model "readfile": ### Failed to generate all binary outputs. The above error will only happen with the following products when certain conditions are met: MATLAB Coder-When the coder is configured to generate a static library-When the toolchain is configured to 'LCC -win32 v2.4.1 | gmake (32-bit Windows)' Simulink Coder-When the model from which code is generated contains model references-When the 'Toolchain approach' is being used to generate a Make file, as opposed to the 'Template Make File' approach-When the selected toolchain is 'LCC -win32 v2.4.1 | gmake (32-bit Windows)'  ?
Workaround: As a workaround, you can do any one of the following:Use another compiler instead of LCC when the code generation process involves the creation of Static Libraries.Manually edit the generated Make file, "filename.mk", and replace all instances of $(RM) in the makefile with "@del -f". Once this is done, you can run the .bat file to generate the binary outputs. ?

Summary: Cannot change "Output folder" setting in Editor publishing options
Description: You can not change the value of the "Output folder" in the Editor's Publishing options panel by browsing to the desired folder using the ellipsis (... ) button.  The options panel does not retain the selected folder.?
Workaround: Manually enter or copy the full output folder path into the text field.

Summary: Importing a spreadsheet with leading empty columns fails and returns an error
Description: Importing data from a spreadsheet fails if the spreadsheet has more than 20 columns, and the left-most columns are empty. MATLAB?returns the following exception at the command line: Exception in thread "AWT-EventQueue-0" java.lang.IndexOutOfBoundsException: Index: 44, Size: 44at java.util.ArrayList.rangeCheck(Unknown Source)at java.util.ArrayList.get(Unknown Source)at com.mathworks.mlwidgets.importtool.WorksheetTableModel.getColumnTargetType(WorksheetTableModel.java:111)at com.mathworks.mlwidgets.importtool.WorksheetTable.updateColumnHeader(WorksheetTable.java:131)at com.mathworks.mlwidgets.importtool.WorksheetTable.createDefaultColumnsFromModel(WorksheetTable.java:411)at com.mathworks.mlwidgets.importtool.WorksheetTable.tableChanged(WorksheetTable.java:336)at javax.swing.table.AbstractTableModel.fireTableChanged(Unknown Source)at javax.swing.table.AbstractTableModel.fireTableStructureChanged(Unknown Source)at com.mathworks.mlwidgets.importtool.AbstractFileTableModel.matlabCallback(AbstractFileTableModel.java:760)at com.mathworks.mlwidgets.importtool.WorksheetTableModel$4$2.run(WorksheetTableModel.java:560)
Workaround: Remove leading blank columns in the spreadsheet before importing.

Summary: Changes to 'Data type override applies to' setting do not trigger data type propagation update in MATLAB Function blocks
Description: When you change the 'Data type override applies to' setting on a model or subsystem that contains a MATLAB Function block, for example, from 'All numeric types' to 'Fixed-point', the data types in the MATLAB Function block do not update. You might see incorrect data types propagated in the model.
Workaround: Make changes to the model that trigger recompilation of the MATLAB Function block. For example, in the Fixed-Point Tool, change the 'Data type override' setting from Double to Single, and then change it back to Double. These changes cause the MATLAB Function block to recompile and update the propagated types.

Summary: Undocumented Simulink Project menu item "Check for Modifications"
Description: In Simulink Project, the context menu has an option "Check for Modifications". This option is not documented. The "Check for Modifications" option contacts the SVN repository to check for external modifications. Simulink Project compares the revision numbers of the local file and the repository version. If the revision number in the repository is larger than that in the local sandbox folder, then Simulink Project displays "(not latest)" next to the revision number of the local file.

Summary: Sensing dynamic variables in SimMechanics Second Generation model results in simulation error
Description: SimMechanics Second Generation models occasionally result in a simulation error. The error occurs in models with Transform Sensor or Joint blocks that output sensing signals for dynamic variables, such as acceleration, force, or torque.
Workaround: To prevent a simulation error, disable sensing for all dynamic variablesˇŞacceleration, force, and torqueˇŞin Transform Sensor and Joint blocks.

Summary: The procedure in the "f for Systems" documentation example gives incorrect interpolation.
Description: The documentation example "f for Systems" shows the following code for interpolating the solution u  to triangle centroids: uintrp = pdeintrp(p,t,u); % Interpolated values at centroidsuintrp = reshape(uintrp,[],N); % matrix with N columnuintrp = uintrp'; % change to row vectors  In fact, the first line is all that is needed, the second and third lines of code scramble the solution.
Workaround: When writing code to interpolate the solution, use only the first line of sample code:uintrp = pdeintrp(p,t,u); % Interpolated values at centroids

Summary: Linearization errors due to a block even though the block linearization is specified
Description: Under some conditions a given block's linearization causes infinite Jacobian matrices even though you have specified a finite value for the block linearization.  When this occurs, linearizing the model returns an error message similar to the following: Error using linearize (line 329)   The Simulink model lin_func_test could not be linearized because the blocks listed below have individual linearizations with A, B, C, or D matrices which are not finite.  Either linearize at another operating point or remove the blocks.  This error can occur when you linearize a model using the linearize  command or using the Linear Analysis Tool.

Summary: MATLAB crashes if you use anonymous functions in mask initialization code
Description: MATLAB crashes when you click "Apply" or "Ok" in the Mask Editor if your mask initialization code uses anonymous functions.
Workaround: Convert anonymous functions in your mask initialization code to regular MATLAB functions.

What() is: ..'   Both mcc  and deploytool  runs into this error. Summary: parfor loops can suffer performance degradation
Description: Some parfor  loops might run more slowly than expected. Not all parfor  loops are affected. To determine whether your code is experiencing this problem, compare execution times when running a parfor  loop on a local matlabpool of size 1 against running the same loop in your desktop MATLAB in -singleCompThread  mode, using the following series of steps: Create a simple function wrapper (myFunction ) around your parfor  loop so you can run it in isolation and make timing comparisons simpler. Open a matlabpool  of size 1 by calling: matlabpool('local',1)  Run your function a few times to let the timings settle. Time your code using: tic, myFunction; toc  Close MATLAB, and restart it using matlab -singleCompThread  Without  opening a matlabpool , repeat the timing procedure: tic, myFunction; toc .  If the code runs significantly slower with a matlabpool  open, it is possible you are encountering this problem.

Summary: 'Device not found' error appears when installing a new board on 32-bit Windows
Description: When a new PCI board is installed using the Install new board  button on the 32-bit Windows operating system, a 'Device not found' error sometimes appears even if the board is present and properly recognized by the software. This is a false error that can be ignored. The model containing the block will load and execute without problems.
Workaround: As a workaround, ignore the false error. An alternative workaround is to uncheck the Auto-detect checkbox in the device driver interface and leave the Device order field at the default value of zero. The false error will not appear.

Summary: Potential segmentation violation on clicking 'View base mask' hyperlink on the Mask Editor for masked linked blocks.
Description: For masked linked blocks, when you click the 'View base mask' hyperlink on the Mask Editor it causes segmentation violation for masked blocks that have more than two levels of masking hierarchy. The segmentation violation is caused if the library is not already open and when you click the 'View base mask' hyperlink on the Mask Editor it tries to open the library. ? ?
Workaround: As a workaround use the 'Go To Library Block (CTRL-L)' menu item to reach the base library block and click the 'View Mask (CTRL-M)' menu item of the library block.

Description: Deployed components built using the MATLAB Compiler or Builder products may crash or hang under certain circumstances.  The errors may include missing DLL errors such as: Caught "std::exception" Exception message is: mkl.dll ... Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Math Function block with natural logarithm function selected may generate incorrect code for complex inputs
Description: Incorrect code is generated by the Math Function block when the following conditions are true:   Math Function block with the Function  parameter set to log  (natural logarithm). The input to the block is a complex number whose absolute value resolves to infinity.

Summary: MATLAB crashes on exit on UNIX OS if "Close"(x) button on desktop window is clicked.
Description: In some cases running on UNIX OS platforms, closing the MATLAB session by clicking the "Close"(x) button on the desktop window title bar (upper right-hand corner in default configurations) causes segmentation violation.
Workaround: Type the quit command from the MATLAB prompt.

Summary: Incorrect fzero example.
Description: The fzero  example "Root Starting From an Interval" has a typo. Instead of fun = @cos; % functionx = [1 2]; % initial intervalx = fzero(fun,x0)  the second line should be x0 = [1 2]; % initial interval

Summary: Integer overflow not reported for fixed-point types with nonstandard word length
Description: For fixed-point types with nonstandard word length (i.e. word length that is not 8-bit, 16-bit, or 32-bit), design error detection for integer overflow does not report overflow. The overflow checks for fixed-point types with nonstandard word length are silently ignored with no indication to the user.

Summary: The distance function may error for antipodal points on the sphere
Description: When the distance  function is used to calculate the distance between certain pairs of antipodal points on a sphere, the following error is thrown:Error using atan2Inputs must be real.Error in distance>greatcircledist (line 159)a = sin((lat2-lat1)/2).^2 + cos(lat1) .* cos(lat2) .* sin((lon2-lon1)/2).^2;Error in distance (line 101)    rng = greatcircledist(lat1, lon1, lat2, lon2, ellipsoid(1));
Workaround: Log in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Make a backup copy of the file MATLAB/toolbox/map/map/distance.m, where MATLAB refers to the output of the matlabroot command in MATLAB.Edit distance.m, inserting the following line in the greatcircledistance subfunction, immediately after the line on which the haversine a is computed:a(a > 1) = 1;Save distance.mExit and restart MATLAB.You may need to type rehash toolbox at the command prompt to update a stale toolbox cache.

Summary: If a Stateflow chart contains unstructured control flow, code inspection may exit with a segmentation violation
Description: If a Stateflow chart contains unstructured control flow, code inspection exits with a segmentation violation. Stateflow charts containing unstructured control flow are fatally incompatible with code inspection. If you check code inspection compatibility for a model with a Stateflow chart containing unstructured control flows, Simulink Code Inspector compability check Check usage of Stateflow charts > Check that control flows are structured  warns. ?

Summary: Unnecessary checkout of product licenses for Accelerator and Rapid Accelerator simulation modes
Description: Simulation in Accelerator or Rapid Accelerator mode results in the unnecessary checkout of MATLAB Coder and/or Embedded Coder licenses if available. If the licenses are not available, simulation continues without error or warning.

Summary: Login to MathWorks Account fails with the error message "An error has occurred."
Description: After you enter your email address and password, the login fails with the error message "An error has occurred." with no additional information regarding the error. This happens when using a proxy to connect to the Internet with proxy settings defined in Windows Internet Explorer.

Summary: Compiled code might fail if it uses some probability distribution objects
Description: Compiled code that uses certain probability distribution objects might fail to run properly and issue the following error message: Undefined function 'dfgetdistributionsold' for input arguments of type 'char'. This might happen if the compiled code does any of the following: Creates a probability distribution by calling the ProbDistUnivParam  or ProbDistUnivKernel  functions (not by calling the makedist  function) Creates a probability distribution by defining a class that inherits from ProbDistUnivParam  Creates a custom distribution that was defined using dfittool  and the File > Define Custom Distribution  menu item in MATLAB R2012b or earlier (not using the same menu item in MATLAB R2013a)
Workaround: In simple cases it might be possible to include the missing file in the compilation command. For example, to compile the testcomp.m file:mcc -m -N -p stats -v testcomp.m -a private/dfgetdistributionsold.mHowever, in some cases this may reveal additional files that need to be included.Another alternative is to use the makedist function in your own code in place of the old ProbDistUnivParam function. For example, instead ofpd = ProbDistUnivParam('normal',[100 5])usepd = makedist('normal',100,5)A third alternative is to install the patch attached to this bug report, following the instructions below.Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh): cd <MATLAB>unzip <.zip file>Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.*Notes for Windows Systems:If you see ˇ°Error: Access is deniedˇ± when attempting to extract the .zip file, you may need to start WinZip in administrator mode. Summary: Incorrect UNR check leading to an incorrect red NTC
Description: In very rare cases, Polyspace displays a UNR check in the middle of a function and propagates it as a red NTC check. This type of error is specific to the code being verified, so it is not possible to provide an example that reproduces the error. If you see this error, contact Technical Support. If possible, provide the source code and the verification log.
Workaround: Installation instructions for Polyspace server R2012bLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your Polyspace installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your Polyspace installation. By default this is in c:\Polyspace or in /usr/local/Polyspace on Linux systems. This folder is referred to as <POLYSPACE> in subsequent steps. Polyspace_Common and PolyspaceFor<lang>_R2012b are the two principal sub-folders of <POLYSPACE>.Stop Polyspace queue manager service.Download the attached .zip files (split archive) associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <POLYSPACE> folder.Each file with a *_z0x.zip and *_zip.zip suffix have to be unzipped independently and renamed as *.z0x and *.zipMake a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Linux Systems: Type the following in a shell:unzip -l <POLYSPACE>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <POLYSPACE> folder.Linux Systems: Type the following in a shell: cd <POLYSPACE>unzip <.zip file>Check that binaries have appropriate ˇ®read & executeˇŻ permissions.Delete the .zip file in the <POLYSPACE> folder.Restart Polyspace queue manager service.

Summary: Code generation error with variant subsystems using Simulink.Variant objects.
Description: Code generation error observed when Variant Subsystem block uses Simulink.Variant object as: hasRainSensor = Simulink.Variant('Rte_SysCon_hasRainSensor') not_hasRainSensor = Simulink.Variant('~Rte_SysCon_hasRainSensor') This issue is seen from R2012a to R2013a. It is fixed in R2013b.
Workaround: Use Simulink.Variant objects with conditions as:hasRainSensor = Simulink.Variant('Rte_SysCon_hasRainSensor == 1') not_hasRainSensor = Simulink.Variant('Rte_SysCon_hasRainSensor ~= 1')

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect output initialization code generated for an Assignment block with constant sample time when it is inside a For/While-Iterator subsystem
Description: Simulink generates incorrect output initialization code in the model output function for an Assignment block in a Simulink model if both of the following conditions are satisfied: The Assignment block has a constant sample time. The Assignment block is inside a For/While-Iterator subsystem.  In such a scenario, the Assignment block output is first assigned in the model initialize function. It is additionally re-initialized incorrectly in the model output function. To check whether this issue affects your model, run the DetectForBugReport943015.p  script. See the Workaround section below for details. ?
Workaround: Download and unzip the attached attachment_943015_R13b_20130516.zip script to a folder and add the folder to the MATLAB path using the addpath command. Run the script on your model, using the following syntax:ret = DetectForBugReport943015('modelname')For a model that is unaffected by this issue, the script returns an empty array. If this issue impacts your model, the script returns an array of structures with the following fields:AssignmentBlock: An Assignment block which has constant sample time and is inside a For/While-Iterator subsystem.IteratorSubsystem: The For/While-Iterator subsystem which contains the above Assignment blockTo work around this issue, move the Assignment block identified and all of its source blocks outside the corresponding For/While-Iterator subsystem. See example example_943015.mdl and example_943015_workaround.mdl in the zip file.

Summary: MATLAB crashes when dragging a MATLAB Function block from the Library Browser into a Simulink function inside a Stateflow chart
Description: When you drag a MATLAB Function block from the Simulink Library Browser into a Simulink function contained by a Stateflow chart, MATLAB crashes.
Workaround: Use one of the following workarounds:Insert the MATLAB Function block into the Simulink function. Selecting the MATLAB function block and in the Library Browser, select Edit > Add Selected Block (or press Ctrl+I).Drag the MATLAB Function block into an empty Simulink model, then drag the block from the model into the Simulink function.In the MATLAB Command Window, use the add_block function to insert the MATLAB function block. For example:       add_block('simulink/User-Defined Functions/MATLAB Function','/path/to/chart/Simulink Function/Inserted Function Block Name')

Summary: Incorrect red NIP check for partially initialized array of pointers
Description: Consider the following code where an array is partially initialized: int init_array(void) {  int var0=0, var1=1, var2=2;   int *myarray[5] = { &var0, &var1, &var2 }; /* Array is partially initialized */    *myarray[2] = 5;    /* Array element 3 is initialized and points to var2 */  return *myarray[3]; /* NIP should not be red */} All elements of myarray  are initialized. Note that the elements myarray[3]  and myarray[4]  are initialized to 0. The verification should highlight the dereferencing of a NULL pointer with a red IDP check. However, the verification generates a red NIP check on myarray[3] , which is incorrect.

Summary: Setting for GRT model option Generate reusable code is not preserved
Description: If you perform the following actions on a model, the value of the model option Generate reusable code  is not preserved: Open a new or existing model, and open the Configuration Parameters dialog box. On the Code Generation  pane, set the System target file  parameter to grt.tlc . On the Code Generation > Interface  pane, select the Generate reusable code  option. Apply changes. Close the model. Reopen the model.  When you reopen the model, the Generate reusable code  option is no longer selected.

Summary: Upgrade Advisor issues a warning when Check model for block upgrade issues check is run on a Simulink model with a Stateflow chart under certain conditions
Description: A warning message is issued by Upgrade Advisor when Check model for block upgrade issues  check is run on a model with a Stateflow chart having the following settings Has 2 or 3 input ports and 2 output ports Mask  parameter is off  Link Status  parameter is none

Summary: Stop button on toolbar can become unusable after you restore or propagate library links
Description: If you restore or propagate changes to a library linked block while any system inside that block is currently open in a Simulink Editor, then when you start a simulation, the toolbar buttons do not reflect the correct state. Most noticeably, you cannot use the Stop  button.
Workaround: To address this issue:1. Stop the simulation. Either use the Simulation > Stop menu option or at the MATLAB command line, enter:      set_param(modelname,'SimulationCommand','stop')2. Close the model window and reopen it.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: bi2de and de2bi functions fail when the first input is variable-size
Description: When the first input to the bi2de  or de2bi  function is variable-size, the function throws out an error message "Required parameter empty " in code generation. For example, for a function foo.m  defined as function y = foo(x)%#codegeny = bi2de(x);  The following command shows the error: codegen foo -args coder.typeof([1 0 1],[1 8],[0 1]);
Workaround: Edit the function bi2de or de2bi by typing the following in the command window:cd(matlabroot)edit toolbox/comm/comm/eml/bi2de.m (or de2bi.m)In bi2de.m, replace the line 12, which says eml_assert(~isempty(b),'Required parameter empty.'), by the following:if eml_is_const(size(b))eml_assert(~isempty(b), 'Required parameter empty.');elseassert(~isempty(b), 'comm:bi2de:InputEmpty', 'Required parameter empty.');endIn de2bi.m, replace the line 123, which says eml_assert(~isempty(d),'Required parameter empty.'), by the following:if eml_is_const(size(d))eml_assert(~isempty(d), 'Required parameter empty.');elseassert(~isempty(d), 'comm:de2bi:InputEmpty', 'Required parameter empty.');end

Summary: Eye Height measurement overestimates the random noise and gives a smaller measurement value than expected
Description: The commscope.eyediagram object provides eye measurements, including Eye Height and Vertical Eye Opening. Eye Height measurement assume a single Gaussian distribution for the received signal values. This may result in over estimation of the standard deviation and gives a smaller estimated eye height value than you would obtain when you use a dual-Dirac model fit. The dual-Dirac model fit is used for vertical eye opening measurements; however, the results of the fit may not be completely accurate.

Summary: textscan throws "std::exception" message when string or file ends with carriage return character
Description: The textscan  function throws a "std::exception" message when the string or file ends with a carriage return character and no other line ending character precedes it. For example, this code: textscan(sprintf('a\r'), '%s')  Returns the following message: Caught "std::exception" Exception message is: invalid string position'

Summary: The To Video Display block is missing DLL files during code generation for packNGo.
Description: When the To Video Display  block generates code for packNGo, it does not include all required DLLs in the resulting zip file.
Workaround: There are two options you can use to fix the problem for the R2013a version of Matlab.Option-1: Copy the following dlls from your matlabroot\bin\win64 or matlabroot\bin\win32 path to the zip folder:boost_date_time-vc100-mt-1_49.dllboost_filesystem-vc100-mt-1_49.dllboost_system-vc100-mt-1_49.dllicudt49.dllicuin49.dllicuio49.dllicuuc49.dlllibexpat.dlllibmwi18n.dlllibmwresource_core.dllOption-2: Add a From Multimedia File block to your model containing the To Video Display block. Generating code with this additional block will copy all the above mentioned missing DLLs into the zip file.

Summary: For a model which contains selectors with continuous sample time that drive derivative ports, if the model uses an implicit solver whose solver Jacobian method option is not set to 'Full perturbation',simulation can produce incorrect results.
Description: If a model contains selectors with continuous sample time that drive derivative ports, and the model uses an implicit solver, whose solver Jacobian method option is not set to 'Full perturbation', this can cause the simulation to slow down (and possibly not complete) or produce an error message.
Workaround: To avoid this, set the solver's Jacobian method to "Full perturbation".

Summary: Intel version 13.1 compilers cannot be detected by MEX.
Description: MEX cannot detect Intel Composer XE 2013 version 13.1 compilers. If you installed an Intel Composer XE 2013 compiler other than version 13.0, MEX does not list it in the detected compiler list and you cannot choose this compiler to create MEX-files.

Summary: anova1 ambiguity when applied to matrix data with repeated group labels
Description: One syntax of the anova1  function accepts a matrix of data and a set of labels for the columns.  If there are repeated labels and there are no NaN values in the matrix, then the function tests for differences between groups defined by the columns.  If there are repeated labels and there are NaN values in the matrix, the function tests for differences between groups defined by the labels.
Workaround: The proper work-around depends on the test you want to carry out:To carry out a test on column means, regardless of whether the matrix contains NaN values, either omit the labels or insure that the labels are all different.To carry out a test on groups defined by the label values, consider using the form of anova1 that accepts a vector input. Alternatively, you could append an entire row of NaN values to your data matrix.

Summary: If the output of a Data Type Conversion block is input to a Product block performing matrix operations, code inspection can fail
Description: If the output of a Data Type Conversion block is input to a Product block performing matrix operations, and the Loop Unrolling threshold  (RollThreshold ) is less than the signal dimensions, code inspection can fail. The Loop Unrolling threshold  is a configuration parameter set in the Configuration Parameters dialog box, on the Optimization > Signals and Parameters  pane. ? ?
Workaround: Change the loop unrolling threshold so that it is greater than the signal dimensions, regenerate code, and inspect the model. The Loop Unrolling threshold (RollThreshold) is a configuration parameter set in the Configuration Parameters dialog box, on the Optimization > Signals and Parameters pane.

Summary: Segmentation vilotation when using signal object with enumerated data type
Description: You may experience a segmentation violation if your model contains a signal object with enumerated data type. Specifically, the segmentation violation can occur if a signal in your model is associated with a signal object that meets both these conditions: The storage class of the signal object has a value other than Auto . The signal object's InitalValue  parameter is specified.
Workaround: Use one of these options to avoid seeing a segmentation violation:Set the signal object's InitialValue property to ''Set the signal object's StorageClass property to 'Auto'Modify your model so that the signal is associated with a different data type

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Referenced models with a bus object as the return value might result in incorrect simulation and code generation
Description: Referenced models might result in incorrect simulation and code generation if all of the following conditions are met: The Outport block of a conditional subsystem or a function-call subsystem in the model connects to an output port of a Bus Creator block. This Bus Creator block connects to the model's root Outport block and the Outport block has a bus object as the data type. The Function specification  parameter for the model interface is set to Model specific C prototypes  or I/O arguments step method   and the Outport block is the return argument.  The attached example model, mRootOutReturnValue.mdl , includes a referenced model, mChild.mdl , meeting these conditions. Generated code for this model does not produce the same results as the simulation.
Workaround: On the Configuration Parameters dialog box, on the Code Generation>Interface pane, click Configure Model Functions. In the Configure model initialize and step functions subpane, in the row for the Output argument, set the value of Category to Pointer.

Summary: Using the LCC compiler to build a model reference hierarchy that contains a protected model results in an error
Description: When using the LCC compiler to build a top model that contains a protected model, the following type of error is generated: Unable to read file 's:\work\test\slprj\sim\_sharedutils\rtwtypeschksum.mat': no such file or directory.
Workaround: Use another?supported compiler.

Summary: Two-side printing of Simulink model does not work in R2012b and R2013a.
Description: When printing a larger model or chart and the tile option is enabled, double sided printing does not work even when the printer is default to double sided printing.

Summary: Code generation with function execution time profiling causes MATLAB crash
Description: If you generate code from a model that uses blocks from Simulink libraries and has Configuration Parameters > Code Generation > Verification > Measure function execution times  selected, MATLAB might crash. For example, this crash can occur in a software-in-the-loop (SIL) or processor-in-the-loop (PIL) simulation.  The failure occurs when the software tries to instrument a function call site included in the library component of the generated code.
Workaround: To avoid this problem, in the model, clear the Configuration Parameters > Code Generation > Verification > Measure function execution times check box. Alternatively, for any block that belongs to a Simulink library, disable the library link.?

Summary: hdrread cannot read images with fewer than eight columns
Description: The hdrread  function incorrectly assumes that High Dynamic Range (HDR) files have at least eight columns. Attempting to load a narrower image will result in the following error:  Error using reshapeTo RESHAPE the number of elements must not change.Error in hdrread>readImage (line 106)    img = reshape(encodedData, [scanlineWidth, scanlineCount, 4]);Error in hdrread (line 29)data = readImage(fid, fileinfo);

Summary: High Dynamic Range (HDR) data shifts more than expected using hdrread and hdrwrite
Description: The hdrread  and hdrwrite   functions use incorrect encoding and decoding rules to convert between the RGB values used in MATLAB and the encoded RGBE values stored in HDR files. As a result, high dynamic range data will shift more than expected during a MATLAB to HDR to MATLAB round-trip using the hdrread  and hdrwrite  functions. (Note: The RGB to RGBE conversion is inherently lossy, and most RGB values will shift going from RGB to RGBE. The bug here is in the degree of change.)

Summary: Nonstandard EtherCAT data types are not handled correctly
Description: If an EtherCAT device defines a data type other than one of the Simulink data types supported by xPC Target, we throw an error and do not accept the configuration file.
Workaround: Installation instructions       Log in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation.       Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.       Exit MATLAB.       Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.                     Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file:                                    Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.                                          Extract the files from the .zip file.                                                               Windows Systems*:                                                             Open the .zip file.                                  Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.                                                                            Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.       After you install the patchA Tx var or Rx var block will now allow you to select a variable that is not one of the standard Simulink types.  As an example, the EL6751 CANopen interface from Beckhoff configuration presents the whole RxQueue as a single variable with bitsize 484 bits (with the default 10 CAN messages configured).  The Rx block now presents an output port with 484/8 = 106 bytes.  The user is responsible for converting these bytes to the individual variables.  An embedded Matlab block can be used for this task.  Use information obtained by looking at the RxQueue structure in ET9000 to figure out what variables are present and what their byte offsets are.  Remember little-endian byte ordering.  The first byte of a 16 bit int is the low-order byte and the second byte is the high-order byte.  *Notes for Windows Systems:    If you see ˇ°Error: Access is deniedˇ± when attempting to extract the .zip file, you  may need to start WinZip in administrator mode. Do this by navigating to  WinZip.exe or the WinZip Start menu entry, right-clicking on it, and selecting  Run as administrator.

Summary: Specifying elements to select or assign in Data Store Read or Data Store Write blocks may generate an error
Description: In Data Store Read and Data Store Write blocks, when you specify a signal whose name contains an "x" as an element to read or assign, Simulink replaces the "x" with a comma. If you click Select , you get an error.
Workaround: Before you click Select, change the comma character in the dialog box to an "x".

Summary: Code inspection Failed to verify status for compatible models containing referenced models inside conditional subsystems
Description: If your model contains a referenced model, and the referenced model is inside a conditional subsystem and uses an internal state, code inspection can fail to verify a compatible model.
Workaround: Consider reconfiguring your model to avoid having a referenced model inside a conditional subsystem.

Summary: Model update generates an error if the Output data type parameter of a Saturation Dynamic block is set to Simulink.AliasType
Description: Model update generates this error if the Output data type  parameter of a Saturation Dynamic block is set to Simulink.AliasType:     Error in '<model>/Saturation Dynamic': Initialization commands cannot be evaluated.   Caused by: No appropriate method, property, or field DataTypeMode for class Simulink.AliasType.

Summary: Difference between AUTOSAR IRV label defined between two runnables and IRV label defined inside runnable might cause MATLAB crash
Description: Given a model configured for AUTOSAR, in which connected function-call subsystems represent connected AUTOSAR runnables, generating code might crash MATLAB if all of the following conditions exist: In the model, a signal line connects two function-call subsystems. The line models an AUTOSAR inter-runnable variable (IRV) connecting two AUTOSAR runnables. Between the two function-call subsystems, the line is labeled, for example, with the name irv1 . Inside the function-call subsystem that is the IRV signal source, the line leading to the outport that corresponds to line irv1  is labeled with a name other than irv1 . (The name neither matches irv1  nor is empty.)  Under these conditions, generating code might crash the MATLAB session. To reproduce this bug with an example model: Open the example model rtwdemo_autosar_multirunnables , and then open the subsystem ASWC . Notice that signal irv1  originates in Runnable1 . Open the subsystem Runnable1 . Inside Runnable1 , add any label other than irv1  to the line leading to the outport Adder . At the top level of the model, generate code for subsystem ASWC  and observe that MATLAB crashes.
Workaround: To work around this bug, open the runnable that is the IRV signal source, and either rename the outport line to match the line connecting the two runnables, or remove the label for the outport line.

Summary: Simulink might give incorrect results for parameter values of data type other than double in the Data Type Propagation block
Description: Simulink might give incorrect results if a non-double value is entered in any of the following parameters of the Data Type Propagation block Number-of-bits: Multiplicative adjustment Number-of-bits: Additive adjustment Slope: Multiplicative adjustment Slope: Additive adjustment Bias: Multiplicative adjustment Bias: Additive adjustment  Note:  Blocks using the Data Type Propagation block within their masks, such as the Transfer Fcn Direct Form II block, might encounter the same issue.
Workaround: To work around this issue, use double data types for the parameter values.

Summary: Using SVN in Simulink Project, Get File Lock fails silently if another user holds the lock
Description: When using SVN source control in Simulink Project, if you try to get a lock on a file by selecting Get File Lock in the context menu, and another user already holds the lock, this attempt fails silently. You do not get a lock on the file, and you do not see any error message to report that the attempt failed. The padlock symbol does not appear in the SVN column next to the file.
Workaround: To work around this problem, look for visual feedback that you successfully obtained a lock. When acquiring a lock, check to see if the padlock symbol appears in the SVN column in Simulink Project file views. If you have locked the file, you can see the padlock symbol.

Summary: griddata function returns incorrect results for 'cubic' or 'v4' methods when tightly clustered points contain Inf or NaN function values
Description: When the griddata  function finds multiple sample points, (x,y) , that are very close together, then it replaces all the similar points with an average of those point coordinates to make a unique point. It also calculates an average function value, v , at the new location. If any of the original values in v  contain Inf  or NaN  values, and you are using the 'cubic'  or 'v4'  methods, then griddata  fails to calculate the averages correctly. This leads to incorrect results that have repeated (x,y)  coordinates but different function values.
Workaround: To avoid this problem, try using the 'linear' method instead of 'cubic' or 'v4'.If linear interpolation does not produce acceptable results, then follow the instructions below to install the attachment.Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. Summary: Incorrect return line pressure loss calculation in Reservoir block
Description: In the Reservoir block, if you use a value other than 1  for the Pressure loss coefficient in return line  parameter, the computed pressure loss is incorrect. The block reference page describes what the correct equation should be, but the block mistakenly uses the inverse of the pressure loss coefficient in this equation.
Workaround: Instead of using the desired pressure loss coefficient as the Pressure loss coefficient in return line parameter value, enter the inverse of the desired value (that is, 1/ploss).Note: Once this bug is fixed, you will have to manually revert any models using this workaround back to the correct value of the pressure loss coefficient. Add this bug to your Watched Bugs List to be notified when it is fixed.

Summary: Simulink Editor operations slow when Model Explorer has a lot of workspace data
Description: If workspace have a lot of data objects of type Simulink.Parameter or Simulink.Signal, common Simulink Editor operations such as moving a blockslow down considerably if either  of these conditions exist: Model Explorer is open with the workspace node is selected in model hierarchy view.  The workspace is not selected, but the Current System and Below  option is selected.
Workaround: To avoid this issue, do one of the following:Make sure workspace is not a current selection in ModelExplorer.If workspace is not a current selection but the workspace has a lot of data, make sure Current System and Below is not selected.

Summary: Import of comments is sensitive to case of source file name
Description: On a Windows operating system, the import of comments  from a previously verified source file will not happen if the name of the latest source file differs in case. Consider the following scenario, where you do the following: Run a verification for sources\import.c . Add comments to a number of checks for the file. For the next verification, before running the verification, change the name of the source file to ImPort.c . At the end of the verification, try to import comments from the previous run.  The software generates a message that states the checks are not found in the "new code" and, therefore, are not imported.

Summary: Compiling a MATLAB function with Image Acquisition VideoDevice System object results in an error
Description: Using Image Acquisition VideoDevice System object with compiler results in the following error Error using VideoDevice/step    Could not connect to image acquisition device.
Workaround: Please contact MathWorks Technical Support team for a workaround.

Summary: Two models configured for external mode simulation with "Wind River Diab/GCC (makefile generation only)" IDE tool chain cannot run simultaneously.
Description: The error occurs in the following situation. Run two external mode simulations for model1 and model2 which are configured to use "Wind River Diab/GCC (makefile generation only)" IDE tool chain with default base rate priority of 40. The two models generate model1.vxe and model2.vxe. Follow the steps below to simultaneously run the two models with external mode simulation. 1) Launch the first model (model1.vxe) with the "-w -tf inf -port <12346>" options.2) Launch the second model (model2.vxe) with the "-w -tf inf -port <12345>" options.3) Press the external mode connect button of model1 in Simulink.4) Press the external mode connect button of model2 in Simulink.5) Run model1 in Simulink.6) Run model2 in Simulink. Step 3 incorrectly starts off the external mode simulation for model1 in addition to the desired connect action. Therefore as per the Step 4, the model2 does not connect to the target as the target is already running.
Workaround: 1. Backup main_vxworks.tlc in folder $(MATLABROOT)/toolbox/idelink/foundation/pjtgenerator/main2. Unzip the attached zip file and copy main_vxworks.tlc to folder $(MATLABROOT)/toolbox/idelink/foundation/pjtgenerator/main.3. You can set the base rate priority in the configuration parameters of the model. Change the base rate priority of the model1 and model2 to more than 50 as the default network task priority in VxWorks is 50.4. Follow the below steps to simultaneously run the two models in external mode.Launch the first model (model1.vxe) with the "-w -tf inf -port <12346>" optionsThe second model should be launched with lower priority. This can be done by running the following command in vxsim.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   rtpSp("host:X:/model2.vxe -w -tf inf -port 12345", <Lowerpriortynum>)              NOTE: The <Lowerpriortynum> number is greater than the base rate priority of model1. This ensures that model2 is launched with a lower priority compared to model1.Press the external mode connect button of model1 in Simulink.Press the external mode connect button of model2 in Simulink.Run model1 in Simulink.Run model2 in Simulink.??

Summary: SVD with three output arguments may fail to converge for certain valid inputs.
Description: [U,S,V] = svd(A)  may return the following error for certain valid inputs.  Error using svd   SVD did not converge.

Summary: Code inspection fails for If blocks containing variables with numeric precisions of more than 4 decimal places.
Description: Code inspection fails for If blocks with If  or Elseif  expressions containing variables with numeric precisions of more than 4 decimal places.  For example, Simulink Code Inspector will fail to verify the block if the If  expression is of the form u1 == val , where val  is defined in the MATLAB workspace as any of the following:  va l  = 3.141592653589793 val   = 3.141592 val  = 31.41592
Workaround: In the If or Elseif expression, replace the variable with its numerical value. For example, if the If expression is of the form u1 == val, where val is defined as 3.141592653589793, replace u1 == val with u1 == 3.141592653589793.

The Discrete State-Space block is connected directly to a root inport. The D  parameter evaluates to 0 in the context of the referenced model.  ?

Summary: lsqlin sometimes gives residuals of the wrong sign.
Description: When you solve a problem that has bound constraints with the default lsqlin  trust-region-reflective algorithm, lsqlin  returns residuals  -(C*x-d)  instead of the documented value C*x-d. lsqlin  returns the correct residuals for unconstrained problems, and for problems that use the active-set algorithm.
Workaround: Reverse the sign of the returned residuals, or use the lsqlin active-set algorithm.

Summary: Symbolic Math Toolbox diag function misses diagonal entries of particular matrices.
Description: The commands A = sym('A_',[2,3]);diag(A,1) return the 1x1 matrix ans =A_1_2 The correct result is the 2x1 matrix [A_1_2; A_2_3]
Workaround: Instead of calling diag on the matrix A, use the MATLAB indexing to extract the corresponding subdiagonal of A:D = reshape(1:numel(A),size(A));Indices = diag(D,1);A(Indices)This returns the correct result:ans = A_1_2 A_2_3

Summary: TreeBagger can produce incorrect predictions if you input class labels Y as a categorical or numeric array
Description: If you input class labels Y  to TreeBagger  as a categorical (ordinal or nominal) array or a numeric array, the order of classes in the ClassNames  property of the TreeBagger  object and the order of the columns in the array of scores returned by the predict  method might be mismatched. As a result, the predict  and oobPredict  methods of TreeBagger  might return incorrect class labels. TreeBagger  methods such as error , oobError , margin , oobMargin  and others would then return incorrect results as well.
Workaround: Convert class labels Y to a cell array of strings before passing them to TreeBagger.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Code from default transitions can sometimes be reused incorrectly for inner transitions
Description: Code from default transitions can be reused incorrectly for inner transitions when the following conditions are met: A state has a single default and a single inner transition. Both the default and inner transitions lead to the same junction. The default and inner transitions have the same leading  actions but are otherwise different. For example, the default transition has the code a=0;b=0;  while the inner transition has the code a=0; .  In this situation, the correct behavior is that when the state is first entered, we execute a=0;b=0;  and when the state remains active, we execute only a=0; . However, this bug results in the state executing a=0;b=0;  not just the first time it is entered, but even in succeeding time steps. This wrong behavior is illustrated in the attached example incorrect_inner_default_reuse.mdl . In this example, the correct behavior is that the output b  gets incremented from b=1  to b=11  during the course of simulation. However, if debugging is turned off, or when code is generated, b  keeps getting reset every time leading to it always having the value 1.
Workaround: As a workaround, make sure that the first actions of the default transition and the inner transition are not identical. For example, consider using b=1;a=1; for the default transition and a=1; for the inner transition.

Summary: Put-call parity is not observed in SWAPTIONBYBLK for non-flat yield curves.
Description: The "put" and "call" price of a swaption should be the same when the Strike  is equal to the forward swap rate. However, the "put" and "call" prices produced by SWAPTIONBYBLK can be significantly different even when the Strike  is equal to the forward swap rate. This does not happen when the yield curve is flat.
Workaround: Download the attached file "swaptionbyblk.m" and replace it for the original on the following path$MATLABROOT\toolbox\fininst\fininst\where $MATLABROOT is the path returned by typing "matlabroot" in the Command Window.
